feature_name: Rich symptom timeline and patient narrative intake
problem_statement: Short, single-symptom forms and rushed histories lose duration, severity, patient concern, prior failed visits, and functional impact before clinician review.
evidence_summary: Patient-flow and clinician packages flag incomplete history and single-symptom routing; patient complaints show dismissal and unexplained symptoms; missed-signal cases tie patient voice/severity capture to delayed diagnosis patterns.
patient_stage: before visit; intake form; medical history; first clinician assessment
missed_signal: duration, change from baseline, patient fear, functional impact, prior attempts to seek care, photos or patient-entered context
why_missed: front-door workflows often sort urgency or route a single symptom rather than synthesize an episode; time pressure and digital forms compress narrative.
proposed_capability: Structured + free-text intake: timeline builder, “what changed from normal?”, “what worries you most?”, impact on daily life, prior visits/tests, patient theory, and optional photos/documents.
data_needed: symptom onset/duration/severity, baseline comparison, patient concern, functional limits, prior visit/test references, language/accessibility needs, consent for attachments
failure_mode: form burden, low-literacy exclusion, clinician note bloat, patient-entered data overinterpreted
safety_guardrail: keep narrative clearly labeled as patient-reported; summarize only clinically relevant deltas; allow clinician review/edit; support translation and accessibility
example_quote_or_case: Patient complaint quote: “I felt completely dismissed and silenced.” Also clinician quote: “Algorithms that go down single symptom routes miss the complexity of general practice…”

problem_statement: Hidden, chronic, fluctuating, and rare conditions may look non-urgent at a single visit unless the system captures deterioration from the patient’s personal baseline.
evidence_summary: Cases around endometriosis, POTS/dysautonomia, ME/CFS functional decline, CKD stories, rare disease delays, and clinician time-pressure sources point to baseline change and function as diagnostic signals.
patient_stage: before visit; intake form; follow-up; long-term monitoring
missed_signal: functional decline, baseline change, fluctuating patterns, recurring impairment
why_missed: single-encounter vitals/labs can look normal; patient function is often not structured or trended.
proposed_capability: Baseline-change module with patient-selected anchors: work/school/activity, sleep, eating/drinking, mobility, self-care, and symptom-free baseline.
data_needed: patient baseline, current function score, trend over time, free-text examples, caregiver corroboration when appropriate
failure_mode: turns subjective experience into crude scores; misses cultural/language nuance; over-alerts common fatigue/pain
safety_guardrail: show trend and examples rather than algorithmic diagnosis; calibrate alerts to persistence, deterioration, and red flags
example_quote_or_case: ME/CFS case quote: “get enough food to live”; Endometriosis reports include long symptom-tracking burden.

problem_statement: Prior cancer, family history, previous diagnoses, and caregiver observations can exist in records or family reports but fail to influence the current diagnostic frame.
evidence_summary: The Brian continuity-of-care case centered on prior cancer plus repeated back pain not being connected; patient-complaint cases include prior cancer and family-history signals ignored.
patient_stage: medical history; first clinician assessment; repeat visit; specialist review
missed_signal: prior cancer/high-risk history, family history, caregiver concern, prior diagnosis context
why_missed: record exists but is not surfaced at the point of triage/assessment; patient/caregiver history is treated as context rather than a diagnostic signal.
proposed_capability: Persistent high-risk history card that attaches to symptom categories and repeat visits; caregiver/family-history prompt when relevant.
data_needed: prior diagnoses, surgeries, cancer history, family history, genetics/rare disease history, caregiver concern, relation and consent
failure_mode: irrelevant old history clutters clinician view; family report may be inaccurate or sensitive
safety_guardrail: rank by current symptom relevance and recency; keep provenance; allow clinician suppression with reason
example_quote_or_case: Brian: severe back pain after prior breast cancer was not connected across multiple contacts; patient/family quote: “No one listened to us.”
internal_evidence_refs: research_evidence_package_health_system_patient_flows_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C020, E020 | research_evidence_package_patient_complaints_identification_failures_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C004, C005, Q004, Q005 | research_evidence_package_clinician_side_diagnostic_pain_points_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C001, E005 | research_evidence_package_global_health_issue_identification_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E021

feature_name: Medication, access, and affordability as diagnostic clues
user: patient; doctor; nurse; CHW; social worker
problem_statement: Medication interruption, inability to pay, lack of transport, or supply/device problems may cause deterioration or block diagnostic completion but are often hidden in clinical notes.
evidence_summary: Missed-signal product opportunities include medication/access as a clue; global and low-resource evidence shows cost, transport, and diagnostic access shape whether conditions are identified.
patient_stage: before visit; medical history; follow-up; referral
missed_signal: stopped medicines, missed tests because of cost/transport, device/supply gaps, affordability barriers
why_missed: patients may feel stigma; EHR fields emphasize prescriptions rather than real access; clinicians may not have time or resources to ask.
proposed_capability: Sensitive access-risk field with options for cost, transport, stockout, insurance, medicine interruption, device shortage, and preferred support referral.
data_needed: medication list, last taken, reason stopped, affordability/transport constraints, local assistance options, consent
failure_mode: privacy breach or patient blame; clinicians receive problems they cannot solve; biased assumptions based on poverty or location
safety_guardrail: ask neutrally; separate from adherence judgment; route aggregate stockout/access data to system managers; show support options
example_quote_or_case: Missed-signal case library includes medication/access-as-diagnostic-clue; global package warns not to blame patients when cost/transport/system delay blocks follow-up.

feature_name: Longitudinal unresolved-problem tracker / diagnostic episode layer
problem_statement: Multiple visits, labs, referrals, messages, and symptoms are treated as separate transactions, so no one sees the unresolved problem as one diagnostic episode.
evidence_summary: All five packages repeatedly identify repeat visits, no follow-up ownership, and no-one-connected-the-dots as black zones; clinician and global packages explicitly recommend a longitudinal unresolved-problem tracker or diagnostic episode layer.
patient_stage: follow-up; repeat visit; specialist review; long-term monitoring
missed_signal: same/worsening symptom over time, repeated presentations, related labs/referrals/messages, persistent symptoms after normal tests
why_missed: records are encounter-centered; ownership changes across GP, ED, out-of-hours, specialist, and admin queues.
proposed_capability: Diagnostic episode object that clusters unresolved symptoms, visits, tests, referrals, patient check-ins, and open questions until a clinician closes or reframes it.
data_needed: encounter IDs, symptom tags, dates, related tests/referrals, open/closed status, owner, patient-reported persistence, clinician uncertainty notes
failure_mode: false clustering, alert fatigue, medico-legal anxiety, privacy across organizations
safety_guardrail: make clusters explainable; allow clinician merge/split/close with rationale; prioritize persistence + deterioration + red flags over volume alone
example_quote_or_case: Brian case: eight-month delay after repeated contacts; missed-signal product opportunity: auto-cluster symptoms/labs/visits/referrals into unresolved diagnostic threads.

problem_statement: Return visits are often handled as new isolated encounters instead of an escalation signal requiring diagnostic reconsideration.
evidence_summary: Jess’s Rule, Brian continuity case, ED/primary-care cases, and Research B tag counts show repeated visits ignored across settings.
patient_stage: arrival; nurse triage; first clinician assessment; repeat visit; emergency escalation
missed_signal: return within days/weeks/months for same or worsening complaint, repeated GP/ED/out-of-hours contacts
why_missed: encounter view hides count and trajectory; different clinicians do not see the full sequence.
proposed_capability: Automatic repeat-presentation banner with prior diagnosis, unresolved symptom, what changed, dangerous alternatives, and required safety-net plan.
data_needed: visit dates, reason codes/free text, symptom match, discharge diagnosis, current severity, prior tests and unresolved items
failure_mode: punishes appropriate returns; creates reflexive over-testing; false matches for unrelated problems
safety_guardrail: frame as “diagnostic reset” not error accusation; permit clinician override; require meaningful similarity/worsening criteria
example_quote_or_case: Jess’s Rule: “three strikes and we rethink”; Brian had repeated contacts before metastatic cancer diagnosis.
internal_evidence_refs: research_evidence_package_health_system_patient_flows_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E021, C021, C020 | research_evidence_package_missed_signals_diagnostic_errors_2026-05-30.tar.gz: M-005, B-006, B-010, B-028, B-030 | research_evidence_package_patient_complaints_identification_failures_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C008, M002 | research_evidence_package_global_health_issue_identification_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E005, E021

feature_name: Normal-test safety net and test-scope explainer
problem_statement: A normal or incomplete test can be mistaken for diagnostic closure even when symptoms persist or the test does not rule out the feared condition.
evidence_summary: Normal-test trap appears across ED, screening, primary care, chronic disease, and missed-signal case libraries; patient complaints include severe pain after normal bloods/X-ray and PE after clear CXR.
patient_stage: lab interpretation; imaging interpretation; diagnosis discussion; discharge; follow-up
missed_signal: persistent/worsening symptoms despite normal result, test age, test limitations, body-region mismatch
why_missed: result becomes closure; patients are told “normal” without a contingency plan.
proposed_capability: For selected tests, show date/scope/limitations, symptom status after result, “what this does not rule out,” and safety-net check-in date.
data_needed: test result, indication, date, symptom status, red flags, follow-up plan, patient notification and understanding
failure_mode: creates panic or overtesting; too much generic caveat text; unclear clinical responsibility
safety_guardrail: clinician-reviewed language; targeted to persistent/red-flag symptoms; patient-facing text uses calm uncertainty, not diagnosis claims
example_quote_or_case: Patient quote after ED discharge: “Still in severe pain and still have no idea.” Research B normal-test trap count: 7 case-library rows.

problem_statement: Abnormal labs, imaging, pathology, microbiology, and screening results can be available or filed but not acted on, communicated, or followed to closure.
evidence_summary: Clinician package reports alert overload and missed abnormal results; patient safety and complaint cases document missed histology, Bence Jones protein, radiology findings, and critical echo follow-up; Research B counts 9 abnormal-result-not-followed-up cases.
patient_stage: lab interpretation; imaging interpretation; discharge; follow-up; specialist review
missed_signal: abnormal result, unexpected significant finding, positive screen, critical finding, patient notification status
why_missed: inbox overload, unclear owner, handoff after discharge/referral, read receipt mistaken for clinical action.
proposed_capability: Ownership ledger: result severity, named owner, due date, acknowledgement, action plan, patient notified, escalation path, closure reason.
data_needed: result metadata, criticality, ordering clinician, responsible team, encounter context, patient contact, next action, closure audit trail
failure_mode: more alerts worsen overload; ambiguous responsibility between ordering and receiving teams; false closure
safety_guardrail: risk-rank by clinical urgency; separate acknowledgement from action; escalation only when owner/deadline/action missing
example_quote_or_case: NZ positive prostate cancer histology not actioned for about six months; D package metric: 29.8% reported personally missed abnormal lab/imaging results delaying care.
internal_evidence_refs: research_evidence_package_health_system_patient_flows_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C024, C025, C032, 04_topic_outputs/maps_or_matrices/black_zones_matrix.csv | research_evidence_package_missed_signals_diagnostic_errors_2026-05-30.tar.gz: M-004, B-011, B-012, B-017, B-018 | research_evidence_package_patient_complaints_identification_failures_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C010, C011, M001 | research_evidence_package_clinician_side_diagnostic_pain_points_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E001, M002, M003, M004, E015 | research_evidence_package_global_health_issue_identification_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E006, E022

problem_statement: Patients may leave ED/inpatient/urgent care before all results return, with no clear owner, timing expectation, or patient-facing plan.
evidence_summary: Research B proposes a pending-test discharge contract; hospital diagnostic excellence and patient complaint cases show discharge and follow-up ownership risks.
patient_stage: discharge; lab interpretation; imaging interpretation; follow-up
missed_signal: test ordered but not resulted, pending culture/pathology/imaging addendum, who will call patient, what symptoms should trigger return
why_missed: discharge ends the visible encounter while diagnostic work remains open.
proposed_capability: Discharge checklist listing pending tests, expected result window, owner, patient contact plan, red flags, and escalation instructions.
data_needed: pending tests, expected dates, owner/team, patient contact method, safety-net red flags, acknowledgement, follow-up appointment/task
failure_mode: alarms patient without interpretation; owner leaves service; result returns after handoff
safety_guardrail: use calm language and explicit contact routes; integrate with ownership ledger; escalation if owner unavailable
example_quote_or_case: Research B cites delayed positive blood culture and critical echo lost to follow-up cases for pending-test discharge contracts.

problem_statement: A referral being placed is often mistaken for the problem being handled, while real-world referrals can be rejected, delayed, sent to the wrong specialty, missing information, unaffordable, or never attended.
evidence_summary: Referral-loop failure is a high-confidence theme across clinician, patient-flow, global, and complaint packages; patient stories include rejected ADHD referral and wrong-specialty rectal cancer loop.
patient_stage: referral; specialist review; follow-up; repeat visit
missed_signal: referral state after placement, acceptance/rejection, missing info, booking, attendance, result returned, patient confusion
why_missed: systems track “referral sent” but not completion; responsibility shifts between referrer, specialist, admin, and patient.
proposed_capability: Referral state machine: drafted, sent, received, triaged, more-info-needed, rejected, booked, attended, report returned, next-step closed; patient-visible explanation.
data_needed: referral request, service, reason, triage status, booking date, patient barriers, report, owner, escalation thresholds
failure_mode: local referral statuses differ; patient blamed for nonattendance; specialists overwhelmed by escalations
safety_guardrail: distinguish system delay/cost/transport from patient choice; local-configurable statuses; escalation by risk and elapsed time
example_quote_or_case: Clinician metric cites referral-loop failure-rate range of 65–73%; patient case: adult ADHD referral rejected without meeting patient; young rectal cancer patient routed through wrong specialty loop.

problem_statement: Patient or family concern about deterioration may not reliably change triage level or trigger senior review, especially after discharge or during inpatient deterioration.
evidence_summary: Patient complaint and global packages include family concerns and sepsis escalation; England’s Martha’s Rule is listed as a patient-safety initiative; Brian and scan cases include families trying to get clinicians to listen.
patient_stage: follow-up; discharge; inpatient ward; emergency escalation; repeat visit
missed_signal: family/caregiver concern, “not right,” worsening after discharge, deterioration despite reassurance
why_missed: concerns are not structured as actionable tasks; families lack a clear escalation channel.
proposed_capability: Escalation button/check-in that routes deterioration concern to the right clinical review tier with context, recent tests, and safety-net instructions.
data_needed: patient/caregiver concern, symptom change, recent encounter/result/referral, current red flags, contact preference, escalation level and response time
failure_mode: overuse causes triage burden; patients think it guarantees urgent care; response delays create liability
safety_guardrail: clear emergency instructions; triage rules and response times; audit unanswered escalation; avoid replacing emergency services
example_quote_or_case: Patient/family quotes include “No one listened to us” and “He should have been in hospital.”

feature_name: Stage-specific red-flag prompts and emergency pathway timers
problem_statement: Time-sensitive red flags can be missed at arrival, triage, first assessment, discharge, or resource-limited community assessment.
evidence_summary: ED/urgent-care workflows, ESI, urgent-care red-flag policies, IMCI/iCCM danger signs, and Research B emergency cases support stage-specific red-flag prompting.
patient_stage: arrival; nurse triage; vitals; first clinician assessment; labs ordered; imaging ordered; discharge
missed_signal: chest pain, shortness of breath, focal deficits, sepsis danger signs, torsion symptoms, abnormal vitals, pediatric danger signs
why_missed: first handler may be non-clinical/under-resourced; red flags compete with volume; atypical presentations look benign.
proposed_capability: Configurable red-flag module by setting: ED/UC timers, serial ECG/troponin reminders, sepsis reassessment, ultrasound/CTA prompts, CHW danger-sign escalation.
data_needed: presenting symptoms, vitals, age/risk state, red-flag answers, timestamps, ordered tests, reassessment times, escalation decision and rationale
failure_mode: unsafe algorithmic triage if treated as diagnosis; alert fatigue; inappropriate local pathway assumptions
safety_guardrail: clinician override with rationale; local governance; audit false positives/negatives; show uncertainty and time-critical rationale
example_quote_or_case: Research B emergency pathway cases include pediatric sepsis, STEMI after missing ECG, stroke, and torsion; ED review shows top high-harm conditions concentrate ED diagnostic harm.

feature_name: Cross-specialty pattern map for unresolved multi-system symptoms
problem_statement: Symptoms that span GI, gynecology, cardiology, neurology, mental health, or rare-disease domains can be bounced between specialties without a shared unresolved-problem view.
evidence_summary: Missed-signal cases include endometriosis, PSVT misattributed to panic, Crohn’s/gender bias, LAM, and myasthenia; patient complaints include wrong specialty routing for rectal cancer.
patient_stage: referral; specialist review; repeat visit; diagnosis discussion
missed_signal: multi-system symptom cluster, specialty handoff gaps, adjacent specialty not considered, patient history split by organ system
why_missed: specialists receive narrow referral questions; primary care lacks a cross-specialty pattern summary; mental-health or demographic labels anchor interpretation.
proposed_capability: Unresolved-pattern view that clusters symptoms and prior specialty conclusions, suggests missing specialty domains/questions, and provides a shared handoff summary.
data_needed: symptom categories, prior specialist notes, tests/results, unresolved questions, referral outcomes, patient concern, contraindications, local services
failure_mode: inaccurate specialty suggestions; over-referral; undermines specialist judgment
safety_guardrail: present as “questions to consider,” not diagnosis; local referral rules; track outcomes to calibrate
example_quote_or_case: Research B cross-specialty referral map cites B-002, B-003, B-024, B-029, B-032; patient case C015 involved a young rectal cancer patient in a wrong-specialty loop.

problem_statement: Clinicians face time pressure, EHR burden, alert overload, note bloat, and scattered prior data while trying to identify hidden or complex conditions.
evidence_summary: Clinician package documents EHR alert overload, time pressure, note bloat, and scattered screens; patient-flow/global packages call for cross-episode views and explicit unresolved-problem tracking.
patient_stage: first clinician assessment; medical history; specialist review; repeat visit
missed_signal: prior unresolved issues, abnormal labs, trends, referrals, patient concern, prior failed diagnoses, uncertainty
why_missed: information exists but is not prioritized by diagnostic relevance; copied-forward notes and alerts bury signal.
proposed_capability: One-page diagnostic episode summary: timeline, red flags, abnormal/pending results, trend deltas, repeat visits, current patient concern, missing data checklist, and open questions.
data_needed: problem clusters, encounters, notes, lab trends, imaging results, referrals, pending tasks, patient intake, clinician uncertainty, prior diagnoses
failure_mode: summary omits critical nuance; hallucinated/incorrect summarization; clinician overtrust
safety_guardrail: source-linked summary lines; show recency/provenance; allow drill-down; mark uncertainty and unreviewed patient data
example_quote_or_case: Clinician quote: “More alerts than they could effectively manage”; emergency clinician/commentary: “see what my screen looks like.”

problem_statement: Key data needed for diagnostic synthesis—family history, prior tests, medication changes, red flags, physical exam limitations, interpreter needs—may never be collected or reviewed.
evidence_summary: Patient-flow black-zone matrix highlights incomplete history; online consultation tools can lose complexity; complaints show family history and physical examination gaps; telehealth/virtual care has physical exam limitations.
patient_stage: intake form; medical history; physical exam; telehealth; first clinician assessment
missed_signal: family history, prior tests, medication access, red flags, physical exam limitation, interpreter need, patient’s main fear
why_missed: forms are generic; encounter time is short; care navigation and telehealth separate data collection from clinician reasoning.
proposed_capability: Dynamic checklist generated from setting, symptom cluster, prior records, and patient-entered concerns: “ask/verify before closure.”
data_needed: symptom category, visit type, demographics only where clinically justified, prior data, missing fields, local pathway requirements
failure_mode: checklist fatigue; biased rules; creates rigid medicine; misses free-text signals
safety_guardrail: short, explainable prompts; no hard diagnosis; clinician can mark not applicable; include narrative review reminder
example_quote_or_case: Patient case C005: family history/caregiver input can be diagnostic signal; HSSIB GP quote warns single-symptom algorithms miss complexity.

problem_statement: Important new information can be buried in copied-forward documentation and long notes, making it harder to notice change over time.
evidence_summary: Clinician package identifies note bloat and documentation burden as a workflow obstacle and recommends note-diff/copy-forward detection.
patient_stage: medical history; first clinician assessment; follow-up; specialist review
missed_signal: new/worsened symptom, changed lab trend, unresolved issue hidden in unchanged note text
why_missed: EHR documentation emphasizes completeness and billing; copied text obscures fresh signal.
proposed_capability: Highlight “what changed since last visit” across notes, patient text, labs, meds, and referrals; flag excessive copy-forward in diagnostic episodes.
data_needed: prior notes, current note, timestamps, structured data deltas, problem list changes, patient-entered updates
failure_mode: privacy/comparison errors; clinician distrust if diff is noisy; false accusations of copy-forward
safety_guardrail: frame as navigation aid; show exact sources; avoid punitive language; let clinicians dismiss irrelevant deltas
example_quote_or_case: Clinician package product implication: “Provide note-diff and copied-forward detection.”

feature_name: Patient voice protection and visit-summary correction
problem_statement: Patients report being dismissed, not listened to, or receiving unclear explanations; positive examples show listening, explanation, and whole-person context can improve trust and signal capture.
evidence_summary: Patient complaint package contains 20 patient/clinician/public quotes, including dismissal and positive counter-patterns; patient-flow and missed-signal packages echo self-advocacy and patient-voice failures.
patient_stage: diagnosis discussion; discharge; follow-up; patient portal
missed_signal: patient’s own words, unresolved concerns, incorrect summary, dismissed symptom without explanation, deterioration concern
why_missed: clinical notes are clinician-authored; patients often cannot correct misunderstandings quickly; summary portals can obscure diagnosis status.
proposed_capability: Patient voice ledger: verbatim concern quote, “what matters most,” what was addressed/not addressed, patient correction request, and clinician response/status.
data_needed: patient quote, concerns list, visit summary, unresolved concerns, correction request, clinician response, timestamps, privacy preferences
failure_mode: adversarial tone; clinician workload; inappropriate or stigmatizing patient language copied into record
safety_guardrail: respectful language normalization with original preserved separately; triage correction urgency; clinician response workflow; privacy review
example_quote_or_case: Negative quote: “I felt completely dismissed and silenced.” Positive quotes: “Gave me time whilst he listened”; “Whole person, not just the pain.”

product_area: Safety and ethics; Patient voice protection
problem_statement: Symptoms may be minimized because of age, gender, mental-health labels, anxiety/stress explanations, diagnostic overshadowing, or chronic illness stigma before dangerous physical alternatives are reviewed.
evidence_summary: Research B counts mental-health misattribution and symptom dismissal; clinician package cites diagnostic overshadowing; patient complaints include “too young,” “normal part of womanhood,” and anxiety used against patient.
patient_stage: first clinician assessment; medical history; referral; diagnosis discussion
missed_signal: persistent physical symptoms under psychiatric/age/gender label, red flags, functional decline, patient/caregiver concern
why_missed: anchoring, stereotypes, time pressure, difficult-patient labels, normal-test reassurance.
proposed_capability: Guardrail prompt when closing under anxiety/stress/age/gender/functional label: physical red flags reviewed, explanation documented, follow-up/safety net set, patient concern addressed.
data_needed: diagnostic/visit labels, symptom persistence, red flags, patient quote, prior visits, follow-up plan, clinician rationale
failure_mode: stigmatizes clinicians or mental health conditions; checkbox compliance; biased trigger rules
safety_guardrail: focus on coexisting physical risk; non-punitive wording; audit equity outcomes; include mental-health care as valid care, not exclusion
example_quote_or_case: Quotes include “all in your head,” “Too young to have cancer,” and “Normal part of womanhood.” Clinician source defines diagnostic overshadowing.

problem_statement: Language barriers, cultural safety, racism, mistrust, and migration/Indigenous context affect whether symptoms are voiced, believed, escalated, and followed up.
evidence_summary: Global package high-confidence findings say language access and cultural safety affect diagnostic identification; metrics include 32% needed services not accessed for cultural reasons in an Australian source.
patient_stage: before visit; intake form; medical history; referral; follow-up
missed_signal: preferred language, interpreter need, cultural safety concern, mistrust, prior discrimination, migration/Indigenous health context
why_missed: fields are treated as administrative or experience data rather than diagnostic safety data.
proposed_capability: Language/cultural-safety profile attached to diagnostic episode: interpreter mode, translated intake, trust concerns, culturally safe escalation contacts, and consented community/caregiver support.
data_needed: preferred language, interpreter requirement, communication preferences, cultural safety notes, consent, translated patient materials, access barriers
failure_mode: privacy harm; stereotypes; fields ignored; interpreter shortage not solved by software
safety_guardrail: patient-controlled visibility where possible; do not infer ethnicity/culture; track whether interpreter was actually provided; local governance with communities
example_quote_or_case: Global package: “Language access and cultural safety affect diagnostic identification, not only patient experience.”

feature_name: Low-resource diagnostic availability and provenance layer
user: CHW; nurse; doctor; system manager; patient
problem_statement: In low-resource settings, missed identification may result from unavailable tests, stockouts, unreliable equipment, transport/cost barriers, or lack of referral capacity—not only clinician reasoning.
evidence_summary: Clinician/global packages cite diagnostic access gaps; Research B counts low-resource diagnostic supply gaps; patient-flow package includes HWC, IMCI/iCCM, Uganda triage, and CHW pathways.
patient_stage: labs ordered; imaging ordered; referral; follow-up; home/community visit
missed_signal: test unavailable, stockout, equipment quality, sample provenance, referral alternative, counter-referral, affordability
why_missed: the chart may only show that a test was not done, not why or what fallback plan exists.
proposed_capability: Resource-aware layer: mark test availability/provenance, stockout, fallback plan, referral alternative, and supply-chain/manager escalation.
data_needed: facility capabilities, stock status, test source, device/lab quality, cost/transport barriers, referral options, fallback safety-net plan
failure_mode: blames clinic for infrastructure gaps; stale stock data; unsafe fallback suggestions
safety_guardrail: non-punitive infrastructure reporting; timestamped availability; clinician/local-protocol review; offline mode
example_quote_or_case: Global metric: 47% of world population with little or no diagnostic access; Research E Tanzania cancer pathway median delay 358 days from first symptom to diagnosis.

user: CHW; informal provider; nurse; doctor; public-health admin
problem_statement: Community health workers and informal/non-allopathic providers are often first-contact nodes, but their observations and referral attempts may not enter the formal diagnostic record.
evidence_summary: Global package includes India TB referral intervention, rural India CHW cancer detection, WHO RAcE/iCCM, and Brazil Family Health Strategy; patient-flow package includes HWC, iCCM, and home/community pathways.
patient_stage: before visit; home/community visit; referral; follow-up
missed_signal: home observations, danger signs, community screening, informal-provider referral, household/social risk, failure to reach referral facility
why_missed: formal systems often begin at clinic registration; CHW/informal records can be paper/offline/disconnected.
proposed_capability: CHW/informal referral capture: minimal offline form, danger signs, referral reason, photo/document capture, referral receipt, counter-referral/result return.
data_needed: CHW/informal provider ID, household/patient consent, symptoms/danger signs, screening result, referral facility, attendance, outcome, follow-up task
failure_mode: untrained users overburdened; privacy risks in shared devices; referral capacity absent
safety_guardrail: scope-of-practice limits; supervision; offline encryption; referral backup before assigning responsibility
example_quote_or_case: Global metric: non-allopathic provider referral intervention reduced TB diagnostic delay by 8.6 days; RAcE/iCCM diagnosed/treated nearly 1.5 million children.

feature_name: Patient-held record import for fragmented systems
problem_statement: Out-of-system care, private/informal providers, paper records, SMS results, and prior scans may be invisible to the clinician seeing the patient today.
evidence_summary: Global package states private/informal/out-of-system care can be invisible and recommends patient-held record import using photos, PDFs, SMS, and manual entry.
patient_stage: before visit; registration; medical history; referral; specialist review
missed_signal: outside labs, scans, discharge summaries, prescriptions, referral letters, SMS results, private/informal care history
why_missed: health systems are fragmented; interoperability and patient access differ by region; patients carry fragmented evidence.
proposed_capability: Secure import of photos/PDFs/SMS/manual records with provenance, translation, OCR/human review flag, and clinician verification status.
data_needed: uploaded files, source/facility, date, document type, patient notes, consent, verification status, extracted key data
failure_mode: incorrect OCR/extraction; forged or mismatched records; privacy leak; clinician overload
safety_guardrail: mark as unverified until reviewed; retain original image/document; show source/date; minimize copied data
example_quote_or_case: Global package product implication: patient-held record import using photos, PDFs, SMS, and manual entry.

user: patient; navigator; primary care; specialist; admin
problem_statement: Positive screening results or abnormal FIT/mammography/cervical/histology signals require diagnostic completion, but patients can disappear between screen, diagnostic test, specialist appointment, and result discussion.
evidence_summary: Patient-flow package includes abnormal FIT navigation, national screening pathways, and screening false-reassurance; patient complaint and missed-signal packages include delayed cancer after missed smear/histology.
patient_stage: public health screening; lab interpretation; referral; follow-up; diagnosis discussion
missed_signal: positive screen, non-returned kit, colonoscopy not booked/attended, confirmation checkup, result not communicated
why_missed: screening systems may not own diagnostic completion across primary care, labs, specialists, and patient logistics.
proposed_capability: Screening navigator with closed-loop positive-result tracking, navigator outreach, barriers, appointment completion, results, and symptom warning after negative screen.
data_needed: eligibility, invite/kit status, result, symptom status, navigator contact, diagnostic appointment, barriers, final result and follow-up plan
failure_mode: negative-screen false reassurance; contact fatigue; privacy concerns around outreach
safety_guardrail: clear patient language: screening is not diagnosis; targeted navigator escalation; protect outreach privacy
example_quote_or_case: A patient complaint package case reports positive prostate cancer histology not actioned for about six months; B case B-019 concerns terminal cervical cancer after missed smear tests.

feature_name: Diagnosis-status clarity and “not yet explained” state
problem_statement: Patients can leave portals or visits believing a diagnosis was ruled out, confirmed, or ignored when the clinician actually considers it unresolved or pending.
evidence_summary: Patient complaints include “still no idea,” no initial explanation, rejected referral without meeting patient, and product implications call for diagnosis-status clarity in portals.
patient_stage: diagnosis discussion; discharge; follow-up; patient portal
missed_signal: uncertainty, pending tests, unresolved symptoms, differential not closed, diagnosis status mismatch
why_missed: portal labels and discharge summaries often flatten uncertainty; patients may not know what remains open.
proposed_capability: Problem status labels: confirmed, suspected, ruled out for now, not yet explained, pending test/referral, watchful waiting with trigger to return; patient can ask for clarification.
data_needed: diagnosis/problem status, clinician rationale, pending items, safety-net plan, patient acknowledgement, clarification request
failure_mode: creates false reassurance or anxiety; clinicians reluctant to document uncertainty; patient misunderstands “not yet explained.”
safety_guardrail: standard uncertainty language; clinician-controlled diagnosis labels; patient-friendly explanations; no autonomous final diagnosis
example_quote_or_case: Patient quote: “Still in severe pain and still have no idea.” Another quote: “No one had told me initially.”
internal_evidence_refs: research_evidence_package_patient_complaints_identification_failures_2026-05-30.tar.gz: Q003, Q007, C003, C006, 05_implications/product_or_system_implications.md | research_evidence_package_missed_signals_diagnostic_errors_2026-05-30.tar.gz: 04_topic_outputs/other_outputs/product_opportunities.csv | research_evidence_package_clinician_side_diagnostic_pain_points_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E009

feature_name: Risk-ranked result/referral worklists to reduce alert fatigue
problem_statement: Adding reminders can worsen clinician alert fatigue unless the system prioritizes meaningful diagnostic risk, ownership, and actionability.
evidence_summary: Clinician package reports median 63 EHR alerts/day and missed-result risk; risks notes warn more alerts can worsen alert fatigue; referral/result follow-up evidence supports risk-ranked queues.
patient_stage: lab interpretation; referral; follow-up; specialist review
missed_signal: high-risk abnormal result, overdue referral, unresolved severe symptom, repeated returns buried among low-value alerts
why_missed: inbox/worklist volume and non-actionable notifications compete for attention.
proposed_capability: Unified risk-ranked worklist for abnormal results, referrals, pending tests, and unresolved episodes, using criticality, elapsed time, deterioration, and patient vulnerability/access risk.
data_needed: task type, criticality, elapsed time, owner, patient notified, symptom persistence, comorbidity/risk context, referral status, override/closure reason
failure_mode: opaque prioritization; missed low-probability high-harm case; alert fatigue persists
safety_guardrail: transparent ranking factors; local calibration; monitor false positives/false negatives; keep unranked search/filter view
example_quote_or_case: D package metrics: median 63 alerts/day; 55.6% said notification system made it possible to miss test results; 29.8% personally missed abnormal lab/imaging results delaying care.
internal_evidence_refs: research_evidence_package_clinician_side_diagnostic_pain_points_2026-05-30.tar.gz: E001, M002, M003, M004, 05_implications/risks_and_safety_notes.md | research_evidence_package_health_system_patient_flows_2026-05-30.tar.gz: C024, C025 | research_evidence_package_missed_signals_diagnostic_errors_2026-05-30.tar.gz: M-004, M-008, M-009

feature_name: Symptom-still-present check-ins after normal tests or watchful waiting
problem_statement: Safety-netting relies on patients knowing when/how to re-contact care and on systems noticing that symptoms persist after reassurance.
evidence_summary: Safety-netting, normal-test trap, repeated visits, and patient complaints repeatedly show persistent symptoms after a normal test or discharge can be lost.
patient_stage: diagnosis discussion; discharge; follow-up; repeat visit
missed_signal: symptom persistence, worsening, new red flag, patient still lacks explanation, failure to attend follow-up
why_missed: normal result closes task; follow-up is passive; patient may not know which symptoms matter.
proposed_capability: Timed check-in asking whether key symptoms resolved/worsened, with red-flag routing, patient-friendly return instructions, and clinician review if unresolved.
data_needed: symptom list, test/visit date, safety-net instructions, check-in date, patient response, escalation threshold, clinician owner
failure_mode: message fatigue; emergency symptoms routed through slow channel; patient anxiety
safety_guardrail: always show emergency instructions; limit to selected episodes; escalate by red flag/persistence; make owner explicit
example_quote_or_case: Cancer safety-netting and patient complaints both show unresolved severe symptoms after negative/normal tests need active follow-up.
